questions.questionText: Glycogen is best described as:
options: The storage form of carbohydrates in animals
The storage form of proteins in humans
The main circulating lipid in blood
A nucleotide used for DNA synthesis
questions.explanation: Glycogen is the major storage form of carbohydrate in animals. It allows the body to store glucose in a compact, rapidly mobilizable form.

questions.questionText: Blood glucose can be obtained from which three primary sources?
options: Ketone bodies, cholesterol, and bile acids
DNA breakdown, RNA breakdown, and heme degradation
Urea cycle, TCA cycle, and protein synthesis
questions.explanation: Blood glucose comes from dietary carbohydrates after meals, glycogen breakdown during early fasting, and gluconeogenesis during longer fasting when glycogen becomes depleted.

questions.questionText: Glycogenolysis is especially useful because it:
options: Slowly synthesizes glucose from amino acids
Converts glucose into fatty acids only
questions.explanation: Glycogenolysis provides a fast response when blood glucose begins to fall or when muscle needs rapid ATP. Gluconeogenesis can sustain glucose production, but it is slower.

questions.questionText: The main body stores of glycogen are found in:
questions.explanation: The two major glycogen stores are skeletal muscle and liver. They serve different purposes: muscle glycogen supports muscle energy needs, while liver glycogen supports blood glucose maintenance.

questions.questionText: The main function of muscle glycogen is to:
options: Supply glucose to the brain through blood
questions.explanation: Muscle glycogen is a local fuel reserve. During contraction, muscle breaks down glycogen to glucose-6-phosphate, which enters glycolysis to produce ATP.

questions.questionText: The main function of liver glycogen is to:
options: Provide ATP only for liver contraction
questions.explanation: Liver glycogen is used to release free glucose into blood, especially during the early stages of fasting. This helps maintain blood glucose for glucose-dependent tissues.

questions.questionText: Liver glycogen is especially important during:
questions.explanation: During early fasting, liver glycogen is rapidly broken down to maintain blood glucose before gluconeogenesis becomes the major glucose source.

questions.questionText: Glycogen makes up approximately what percentage of the fresh weight of resting muscle?
questions.explanation: Resting muscle contains about 1–2% glycogen by fresh weight. Even though this percentage is low, total muscle glycogen is large because total muscle mass is high.

questions.questionText: Glycogen makes up approximately what percentage of the fresh weight of a well-fed adult liver?
questions.explanation: A well-fed adult liver may contain about 10% glycogen by fresh weight. This is higher percentage-wise than muscle.

questions.questionText: Total glycogen amount is greater in muscle than liver because:
options: Total muscle mass is much greater than liver mass
Muscle has a higher glycogen percentage than liver
Muscle releases free glucose into blood
questions.explanation: Although liver has a higher glycogen percentage, skeletal muscle represents a much larger total mass. Therefore, total body muscle glycogen exceeds liver glycogen.

questions.questionText: Glycogen is made entirely of:
questions.explanation: Glycogen is a branched homopolysaccharide made entirely of α-D-glucose. “Homo” means it contains one type of monosaccharide unit.

questions.questionText: The main glycosidic linkage in linear glycogen chains is:
questions.explanation: Most glucose residues in glycogen are connected by α(1→4) glycosidic bonds, forming the linear chains.

questions.questionText: Glycogen branch points are formed by:
questions.explanation: Branch points in glycogen contain α(1→6) glycosidic bonds. These branches occur after an average of 8–10 glucosyl residues.

questions.questionText: Branches in glycogen occur approximately every:
questions.explanation: The lecture states that after an average of 8–10 glucosyl residues, there is a branch containing an α(1→6) linkage.

questions.questionText: The highly branched structure of glycogen is useful because it:
options: Provides many sites for rapid glycogen synthesis and breakdown
Makes glycogen insoluble in all tissues
Allows glucose to diffuse freely through membranes
questions.explanation: Branching creates many non-reducing ends. Enzymes can work at many ends simultaneously, allowing rapid glycogen synthesis and degradation.

questions.questionText: Glycogenesis means:
questions.explanation: Glycogenesis is the pathway that synthesizes glycogen from glucose-derived activated glucose units.

questions.questionText: Glycogenesis occurs in the:
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthesis occurs in the cytosol. It uses glucose-6-phosphate, glucose-1-phosphate, and UDP-glucose as intermediates.

questions.questionText: Glycogenesis requires energy supplied by:
questions.explanation: ATP is used for phosphorylation of glucose, while UTP is used to form UDP-glucose, the activated glucose donor for glycogen synthesis.

questions.questionText: In glycogenesis, ATP is used mainly for:
questions.explanation: The first step converts glucose to glucose-6-phosphate using ATP. This step is catalyzed by hexokinase or glucokinase depending on the tissue.

questions.questionText: UDP-glucose is best described as:
options: Activated glucose used for glycogen synthesis
Free glucose released from branch points
A product of glycogen breakdown only
questions.explanation: UDP-glucose is the activated glucose donor. All glucosyl residues added to the growing glycogen molecule come from UDP-glucose.

questions.questionText: UDP-glucose is formed from glucose-1-phosphate and:
questions.explanation: UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase uses glucose-1-phosphate and UTP to form UDP-glucose and PPi.

questions.questionText: The enzyme that forms UDP-glucose is:
questions.explanation: UDP-glucose pyrophosphorylase catalyzes formation of UDP-glucose, the activated glucose donor used by glycogen synthase.

questions.questionText: Glucose-6-phosphate is converted to glucose-1-phosphate by:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglucomutase interconverts glucose-6-phosphate and glucose-1-phosphate. This reaction is needed in both glycogenesis and glycogenolysis.

questions.questionText: Glycogen synthase forms which type of linkage?
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase elongates glycogen chains by adding glucose residues through α(1→4) linkages at non-reducing ends.

questions.questionText: Glycogen synthase cannot:
options: Start glycogen synthesis from free glucose
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase can elongate an existing glycogen primer but cannot start chain synthesis from free glucose. A primer is required.

questions.questionText: When glycogen stores are depleted, the primer is provided by:
questions.explanation: If no glycogen fragment is available, glycogenin serves as the acceptor protein for the first glucose residues and initiates primer formation.

questions.questionText: Glycogenin attaches the first glucose residue to a specific:
questions.explanation: The OH group of a specific tyrosine residue in glycogenin is the site where the first glucosyl unit is attached.

questions.questionText: Glycogenin catalyzes its own glucosylation, which is called:
questions.explanation: Glycogenin acts as both primer and enzyme. It catalyzes the transfer of glucose residues to itself, a process called autoglucosylation.

questions.questionText: The branching enzyme is also called:
questions.explanation: The branching enzyme transfers a segment of glucose residues from an α(1→4) chain and attaches it through an α(1→6) linkage, creating a branch.

questions.questionText: Branching enzyme transfers a chain of approximately:
questions.explanation: The branching enzyme removes 6–8 glucosyl residues from a non-reducing end and transfers them to form a new α(1→6) branch.

questions.questionText: Glycogenolysis means:
questions.explanation: Glycogenolysis is the degradation of glycogen. It produces glucose-1-phosphate mainly, and free glucose from branch-point residues.

questions.questionText: Glycogenolysis is:
questions.explanation: Glycogen breakdown uses a separate set of cytosolic enzymes, including glycogen phosphorylase and debranching enzyme. It is not simply glycogenesis running backward.

questions.questionText: The primary product of glycogen phosphorylase action is:
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves α(1→4) bonds by phosphorolysis, releasing glucose-1-phosphate from non-reducing ends.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves:
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase removes glucose residues from linear chains by breaking α(1→4) bonds.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase works from the:
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase starts at non-reducing ends and releases glucose residues as glucose-1-phosphate.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase requires which coenzyme?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase requires pyridoxal phosphate, or PLP, as a coenzyme. PLP is derived from vitamin B6.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase stops when how many glucosyl residues remain before a branch point?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase cannot degrade glycogen all the way through a branch point. It stops when four glucosyl residues remain before the α(1→6) branch.

questions.questionText: The structure left when phosphorylase stops near a branch point is called:
questions.explanation: Limit dextrin is the branched structure remaining when glycogen phosphorylase can no longer continue because it is too close to a branch point.

questions.questionText: Branches are removed by:
questions.explanation: Debranching enzyme removes branches after phosphorylase has shortened chains to limit dextrin. It has two enzymatic activities.

questions.questionText: The α(1→6)-glucosidase activity of debranching enzyme releases:
questions.explanation: Most glycogen breakdown produces glucose-1-phosphate, but the single glucose at an α(1→6) branch point is released as free glucose by amylo-α(1→6)-glucosidase activity.

questions.questionText: Which statement best compares liver and muscle glycogen?
options: Liver glycogen maintains blood glucose, while muscle glycogen provides local energy
Muscle glycogen maintains blood glucose, while liver glycogen provides muscle contraction energy only
Both liver and muscle glycogen are used mainly to release glucose into blood
Both liver and muscle glycogen are used only during digestion
questions.explanation: Liver glycogen is mainly used to maintain blood glucose, especially during early fasting. Muscle glycogen is used locally inside muscle cells to produce ATP during contraction.

questions.questionText: Why is dietary carbohydrate not always a reliable source of blood glucose?
options: Food intake is intermittent, so glucose must also come from stored or newly synthesized sources
Dietary carbohydrate cannot raise blood glucose
questions.explanation: Dietary glucose depends on recent food intake. Between meals or during fasting, the body needs glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to maintain blood glucose.

questions.questionText: Why does glycogenolysis respond faster than gluconeogenesis to falling blood glucose?
options: Glycogenolysis mobilizes already stored carbohydrate, while gluconeogenesis must synthesize glucose de novo
Gluconeogenesis is the fastest pathway in early fasting
Glycogenolysis occurs only after glycogen stores are depleted
questions.explanation: Glycogen is already stored as a glucose polymer, so it can be rapidly degraded. Gluconeogenesis is slower because it must build glucose from smaller non-carbohydrate precursors.

questions.questionText: In exercising muscle, glycogen is degraded mainly to provide:
options: Glucose-6-phosphate for glycolysis and ATP production
Free glucose for release into blood
questions.explanation: Muscle glycogen is not mainly used to maintain blood glucose. It is degraded to glucose-1-phosphate, converted to glucose-6-phosphate, and used in glycolysis to produce ATP for contraction.

questions.questionText: Why can liver glycogen release free glucose into blood?
questions.explanation: In liver, glucose-6-phosphate can be dephosphorylated by glucose-6-phosphatase to free glucose. Free glucose can then enter the blood.

questions.questionText: Why can muscle glycogen not directly maintain blood glucose?
questions.explanation: Muscle can break glycogen down to glucose-6-phosphate, but it cannot convert glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose because it lacks glucose-6-phosphatase. Therefore, muscle glycogen is used locally.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains the importance of glycogen branching?
options: Branching creates many non-reducing ends for rapid synthesis and degradation
Branching prevents enzyme access to glycogen
Branching converts glycogen into a protein
Branching makes glycogen unable to store glucose
questions.explanation: Glycogen enzymes act mainly at non-reducing ends. More branches mean more non-reducing ends, allowing faster glucose mobilization and faster glycogen synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which linkage is cleaved repeatedly by glycogen phosphorylase?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase removes glucose residues from linear glycogen chains by cleaving α(1→4) glycosidic bonds through phosphorolysis.

questions.questionText: Which linkage requires debranching enzyme glucosidase activity?
questions.explanation: The single glucose residue remaining at a branch point is linked by an α(1→6) bond. Amylo-α(1→6)-glucosidase activity removes it hydrolytically, releasing free glucose.

questions.questionText: In glycogenesis, glucose is first converted to:
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthesis begins with phosphorylation of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate. This step uses ATP and is catalyzed by hexokinase or glucokinase.

questions.questionText: In glycogenesis, glucose-6-phosphate is converted to:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglucomutase converts glucose-6-phosphate into glucose-1-phosphate. Glucose-1-phosphate is then activated to UDP-glucose.

questions.questionText: Why is UDP-glucose called activated glucose?
options: It has high transfer potential for adding glucose to glycogen
It is free glucose released from the liver
It is the final product of glycogenolysis
questions.explanation: UDP-glucose is activated because the glucose residue is attached to UDP, making it ready to be transferred to the growing glycogen chain by glycogen synthase.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme uses UDP-glucose to elongate glycogen chains?
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to the non-reducing end of glycogen, forming α(1→4) glycosidic bonds.

questions.questionText: Glycogen synthase adds glucose residues to the:
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase elongates chains at non-reducing ends. This is why branching increases the number of sites for rapid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which molecule serves as a primer when glycogen stores are not totally depleted?
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase cannot start synthesis from free glucose. If a glycogen fragment remains, it can serve as the primer for elongation.

questions.questionText: When no glycogen primer exists, glycogenin begins synthesis by attaching glucose to:
questions.explanation: Glycogenin has a specific tyrosine residue with an OH group that receives the first glucose residue. This starts the primer chain.

questions.questionText: After glycogenin forms a short primer, which enzyme continues elongation?
questions.explanation: Glycogenin creates a short α(1→4)-linked glucose chain. Once the primer is long enough, glycogen synthase can extend it.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes branching enzyme action?
options: It transfers a block of glucose residues and creates an α(1→6) branch
It releases free glucose from liver glycogen
questions.explanation: Branching enzyme removes a chain of 6–8 glucosyl residues from a non-reducing end and attaches it to another glucose residue through an α(1→6) linkage.

questions.questionText: Branching enzyme creates new non-reducing ends mainly to:
options: Allow further elongation by glycogen synthase
questions.explanation: After branching, the new non-reducing ends can be further elongated by glycogen synthase. This increases glycogen compactness and metabolic accessibility.

questions.questionText: Which pathway requires ATP and UTP?
questions.explanation: Glycogenesis requires ATP for glucose phosphorylation and UTP for synthesis of UDP-glucose. Glycogenolysis mainly uses inorganic phosphate through phosphorylase.

questions.questionText: Which pathway uses PLP-dependent glycogen phosphorylase?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase is a glycogenolytic enzyme and requires PLP as a coenzyme. It cleaves α(1→4) bonds by phosphorolysis.

questions.questionText: Glycogen phosphorylase uses which mechanism to release glucose-1-phosphate?
questions.explanation: Phosphorolysis uses inorganic phosphate to cleave α(1→4) bonds, producing glucose-1-phosphate instead of free glucose.

questions.questionText: Which product is released from α(1→4) cleavage by glycogen phosphorylase?
questions.explanation: The main product of glycogen phosphorylase action is glucose-1-phosphate. It can then be converted to glucose-6-phosphate by phosphoglucomutase.

questions.questionText: Which product is released from α(1→6) branch hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: The α(1→6)-linked branch residue is removed by glucosidase activity of the debranching enzyme, producing free glucose.

questions.questionText: What is limit dextrin?
options: A branched glycogen remnant left when phosphorylase stops near branch points
The activated glucose donor in glycogenesis
The first glucose attached to glycogenin
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase stops four residues before a branch point. The remaining branched structure is called limit dextrin and must be handled by debranching enzyme.

questions.questionText: Debranching enzyme is described as bifunctional because it has:
questions.explanation: Debranching enzyme first transfers three glucosyl residues to another chain, then removes the remaining α(1→6)-linked glucose by hydrolysis.

questions.questionText: The transferase activity of debranching enzyme transfers how many glucosyl residues?
questions.explanation: The transferase activity removes the outer three glucosyl residues from a branch and transfers them to the non-reducing end of another chain.

questions.questionText: After transferase activity, the remaining single branch glucose is removed by:
questions.explanation: The α(1→6)-glucosidase activity hydrolyzes the branch-point glucose and releases it as free glucose.

questions.questionText: Glucose-1-phosphate from glycogenolysis is converted to glucose-6-phosphate by:
questions.explanation: Phosphoglucomutase interconverts glucose-1-phosphate and glucose-6-phosphate. In glycogenolysis, it converts G1P into G6P.

questions.questionText: In liver glycogenolysis, glucose-6-phosphate is finally converted into:
questions.explanation: Liver contains glucose-6-phosphatase, which converts glucose-6-phosphate to free glucose. This glucose can be released into blood to maintain blood glucose concentration.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why glycogenolysis is not the reverse of glycogenesis?
options: Different enzymes and different chemical mechanisms are used
The same enzymes simply reverse all reactions
Glycogenesis occurs in mitochondria while glycogenolysis occurs in nucleus
Glycogenolysis uses UDP-glucose as its main product
questions.explanation: Glycogenesis uses UDP-glucose, glycogen synthase, and branching enzyme. Glycogenolysis uses glycogen phosphorylase and debranching enzyme. Therefore, it is not a direct reversal.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes the difference between phosphorolysis and hydrolysis during glycogenolysis?
options: α(1→4) bonds are cleaved by phosphorolysis, while α(1→6) bonds are cleaved hydrolytically
Both α(1→4) and α(1→6) bonds are cleaved only by hydrolysis
Both α(1→4) and α(1→6) bonds are cleaved only by ATP
α(1→6) bonds are cleaved by glycogen phosphorylase
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase uses inorganic phosphate to cleave α(1→4) bonds, producing G1P. The debranching enzyme hydrolyzes α(1→6) linkages and releases free glucose.

questions.questionText: Why is glucose-1-phosphate formation from glycogen energetically useful?
options: It saves one ATP compared with starting glycolysis from free glucose
It must be converted to UDP-glucose before energy production
questions.explanation: Glycogen breakdown gives glucose-1-phosphate, which becomes glucose-6-phosphate without using ATP. Free glucose would require ATP for phosphorylation by hexokinase.

questions.questionText: In muscle, glycogen-derived glucose enters glycolysis as:
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase produces G1P, which phosphoglucomutase converts to G6P. In muscle, G6P enters glycolysis to provide ATP.

questions.questionText: In liver, the major reason glycogen can support blood glucose is:
options: G6P is dephosphorylated to glucose by glucose-6-phosphatase
G6P is converted to lactate and exported
Glycogen phosphorylase directly releases all glucose as free glucose
Muscle sends G6P directly to blood
questions.explanation: Glucose-6-phosphate cannot leave cells efficiently. Liver glucose-6-phosphatase removes the phosphate, producing free glucose that can be exported.

questions.questionText: Which tissue difference explains why muscle glycogen serves only local energy needs?
questions.explanation: Without glucose-6-phosphatase, muscle cannot release free glucose into blood. It uses glycogen-derived G6P in glycolysis for its own ATP production.

questions.questionText: During early fasting, which pathway responds rapidly before gluconeogenesis becomes fully active?
questions.explanation: Glycogenolysis quickly releases stored glucose from liver glycogen. Gluconeogenesis becomes more important later because it is slower.

questions.questionText: During prolonged fasting after glycogen depletion, blood glucose depends more on:
questions.explanation: When glycogen stores become depleted, glucose must be synthesized de novo from precursors such as amino acids, lactate, and glycerol.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why a highly branched glycogen molecule can be rapidly degraded?
options: Many glycogen phosphorylase molecules can work at many non-reducing ends
Only one enzyme can act on glycogen at a time
Branching converts α(1→4) bonds into peptide bonds
questions.explanation: Multiple non-reducing ends allow simultaneous enzyme action. This supports rapid release of glucose-1-phosphate during muscle contraction or fasting.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why glycogen synthase needs branching enzyme cooperation?
options: Synthase forms linear α(1→4) chains, while branching enzyme creates α(1→6) branches
Branching enzyme converts G1P to G6P
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase elongates chains with α(1→4) bonds. Branching enzyme creates branch points by forming α(1→6) linkages.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why glycogenin is necessary?
options: Glycogen synthase cannot initiate synthesis without a primer
Glycogenin releases free glucose into blood
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase can elongate an existing glucan chain but cannot start from free glucose. Glycogenin provides the initial primer when glycogen is depleted.

questions.questionText: Which sequence correctly describes glycogenesis?
options: Glucose → G6P → G1P → UDP-glucose → glycogen primer elongation → branching
questions.explanation: This is the correct synthesis sequence: glucose is phosphorylated, rearranged to G1P, activated to UDP-glucose, added to glycogen, and branched.

questions.questionText: Which sequence correctly describes glycogenolysis?
options: Glycogen phosphorylase shortening → limit dextrin → debranching enzyme → G1P to G6P
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase removes glucose residues until it reaches limit dextrin. Debranching enzyme resolves branches, and phosphoglucomutase converts G1P to G6P.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme is responsible for the rate-limiting step of glycogenesis?
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase is the key rate-limiting enzyme controlling glycogen synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme is responsible for the rate-limiting step of glycogenolysis?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase is the rate-limiting enzyme controlling glycogen degradation.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes glycogen synthase regulation by covalent modification?
options: Active when dephosphorylated, inactive when phosphorylated
Active when phosphorylated, inactive when dephosphorylated
Active only when bound to PLP
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase is active in its dephosphorylated “a” form and inactive in its phosphorylated “b” form. This favors glycogen synthesis in the fed state.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes glycogen phosphorylase regulation by covalent modification?
options: Active when phosphorylated, inactive when dephosphorylated
Active when dephosphorylated, inactive when phosphorylated
Active only when UDP-glucose is high
questions.explanation: The opposite is true for glycogen phosphorylase compared with glycogen synthase. Phosphorylation activates phosphorylase and promotes glycogen degradation.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme form is active for glycogen synthesis?
questions.explanation: Glycogen synthase a is the active dephosphorylated form. It promotes glycogen synthesis.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme form is active for glycogen degradation?
questions.explanation: Glycogen phosphorylase is active in its phosphorylated “a” form. This promotes glycogen breakdown.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme activates glycogen phosphorylase through phosphorylation?
questions.explanation: Phosphorylase kinase phosphorylates glycogen phosphorylase b, converting it to the active phosphorylase a form.

questions.questionText: Which signal activates protein kinase A in hormonal glycogenolysis?
questions.explanation: Glucagon or epinephrine activates adenylate cyclase, increasing cAMP. cAMP activates protein kinase A, which promotes phosphorylation events that favor glycogen breakdown.

questions.questionText: Glucagon promotes glycogenolysis mainly in:
questions.explanation: The lecture states that glucagon acts in the liver only. It promotes liver glycogen breakdown to maintain blood glucose during fasting.

questions.questionText: Epinephrine promotes glycogenolysis in:
questions.explanation: Epinephrine acts in both liver and muscle. It supports blood glucose maintenance in liver and rapid ATP production in muscle during stress or exercise.

questions.questionText: Insulin generally promotes:
questions.explanation: Insulin is a fed-state hormone. It activates glycogen synthesis and opposes glycogen breakdown.

questions.questionText: Glucagon generally promotes:
questions.explanation: Glucagon rises during fasting and acts mainly on liver to increase glycogen breakdown and blood glucose release.

questions.questionText: Which condition favors glycogenesis in liver?
questions.explanation: In the liver, glycogenesis increases during well-fed periods when glucose and insulin are available.

questions.questionText: Which condition favors glycogenolysis in liver?
questions.explanation: During fasting, liver glycogenolysis increases to maintain blood glucose concentration.

questions.questionText: Which condition favors glycogenolysis in skeletal muscle?
questions.explanation: During exercise, muscle glycogen is broken down to provide glucose-6-phosphate for glycolysis and ATP production.

questions.questionText: Which condition favors glycogenesis in skeletal muscle?
questions.explanation: The lecture states that in skeletal muscle, glycogenolysis increases during active exercise, whereas glycogenesis begins at rest.

questions.questionText: Which statement best summarizes hormonal control of glycogen metabolism?
options: Insulin promotes glycogenesis; glucagon and epinephrine promote glycogenolysis
Insulin promotes glycogenolysis; glucagon promotes glycogenesis
Glucagon acts mainly on muscle glycogen only
Epinephrine inhibits glycogen breakdown in all tissues
questions.explanation: Insulin is the fed-state storage hormone. Glucagon and epinephrine mobilize glycogen during fasting, stress, or exercise.

questions.questionText: Which allosteric signals favor glycogen synthesis in both liver and muscle?
questions.explanation: Glucose-6-phosphate and ATP indicate substrate and energy abundance. They activate glycogen synthase and inhibit glycogen phosphorylase, favoring storage.

questions.questionText: Excess glucose-6-phosphate allosterically:
options: Activates glycogen synthase and inhibits glycogen phosphorylase
Has no effect on glycogen metabolism
questions.explanation: G6P signals that glucose is available. It favors glycogen storage by activating synthase and inhibiting breakdown.

questions.questionText: Excess ATP allosterically:
questions.explanation: ATP signals high energy availability. In both liver and muscle, it inhibits glycogen phosphorylase and reduces glycogen breakdown.

questions.questionText: AMP activates glycogen phosphorylase mainly in:
questions.explanation: AMP signals low energy during muscle work. It activates muscle glycogen phosphorylase, increasing glycogen breakdown for ATP production.

questions.questionText: Calcium activates glycogenolysis mainly because it is released during:
questions.explanation: Calcium is released during muscle contraction. It activates phosphorylase kinase, promoting glycogen phosphorylase activation and glycogen breakdown.

questions.questionText: Excess glucose inhibits glycogen phosphorylase mainly in:
questions.explanation: The lecture states that excess glucose inhibits phosphorylase in liver. This makes sense because liver glycogen breakdown should slow when blood glucose is already high.

questions.questionText: Which statement best matches allosteric control with tissue need?
options: Muscle AMP activates phosphorylase to meet local ATP demand
Liver AMP is the only signal for blood glucose release
Liver glucose activates phosphorylase during hyperglycemia
questions.explanation: Allosteric control meets local tissue needs. In muscle, AMP rises during energy demand and activates glycogen breakdown to produce ATP.

questions.questionText: Which statement best compares hormonal and allosteric regulation?
options: Hormonal control meets body-wide needs, while allosteric control meets local tissue needs
Hormonal control uses only PLP, while allosteric control uses cAMP
Both are identical and always affect one tissue only
Allosteric control is only for liver, and hormonal control is only for muscle
questions.explanation: Hormones such as insulin, glucagon, and epinephrine coordinate metabolism for the whole body. Allosteric regulators such as AMP, ATP, G6P, calcium, and glucose respond to local cellular conditions.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes phosphorylation effects?
options: Phosphorylation inhibits glycogen synthase but activates glycogen phosphorylase
Phosphorylation activates both glycogen synthase and phosphorylase
Phosphorylation has no effect on glycogen metabolism
questions.explanation: This is a major exam trap. Glycogen synthase is active when dephosphorylated, while glycogen phosphorylase is active when phosphorylated.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes insulin’s effect on covalent modification?
options: Insulin promotes dephosphorylation, activating synthase and inactivating phosphorylase
Insulin activates cAMP-dependent protein kinase A
Insulin activates glycogen degradation in fasting
questions.explanation: Insulin activates phosphatase activity. Dephosphorylation turns glycogen synthase on and glycogen phosphorylase off, promoting glycogen storage.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes glucagon’s effect in liver?
options: It increases cAMP and phosphorylation, promoting glycogen breakdown
It activates glycogen synthase by dephosphorylation
It acts mainly in muscle only
It directly releases glucose from glycogen branches
questions.explanation: Glucagon binds liver receptors, activates adenylate cyclase, raises cAMP, activates PKA, and promotes phosphorylation of enzymes that favor glycogenolysis.

questions.questionText: Which statement best describes epinephrine’s effect?
options: It stimulates glycogenolysis in liver and muscle
questions.explanation: Epinephrine prepares the body for stress or exercise. In muscle, it supports ATP production; in liver, it supports blood glucose availability.

questions.questionText: Which enzyme pair is reciprocally regulated by phosphorylation?
questions.explanation: Phosphorylation turns glycogen synthase off and glycogen phosphorylase on. Dephosphorylation has the opposite effect.

questions.questionText: Which state promotes the dephosphorylated active form of glycogen synthase?
questions.explanation: Insulin dominates in the fed state and promotes glycogen storage by activating glycogen synthase through dephosphorylation.

questions.questionText: Which state promotes the phosphorylated active form of glycogen phosphorylase?
questions.explanation: Glucagon in fasting and epinephrine/calcium during exercise activate phosphorylation cascades that convert glycogen phosphorylase to its active phosphorylated form.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement is most accurate for liver during fasting?
options: Glucagon stimulates glycogenolysis so liver releases glucose to blood
Insulin stimulates glycogenesis and liver releases no glucose
Muscle glycogen is released as free glucose into blood
questions.explanation: During fasting, liver glycogen is degraded. Because liver has glucose-6-phosphatase, it can release free glucose into blood.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement is most accurate for muscle during exercise?
options: Epinephrine, calcium, and AMP promote glycogen breakdown for local ATP production
Glucagon directly stimulates muscle glycogen breakdown
Muscle releases free glucose into blood using glucose-6-phosphatase
Muscle glycogen is mainly used to maintain brain glucose
questions.explanation: Muscle contraction increases calcium and AMP, and epinephrine supports glycogen breakdown. The resulting G6P enters glycolysis to make ATP for contraction.

questions.questionText: Which statement best explains why glycogen metabolism must be reciprocally regulated?
options: To avoid simultaneous strong glycogen synthesis and breakdown
To keep both pathways maximally active at all times
To convert glycogen directly into fat only
To prevent glucose storage during fed state
questions.explanation: If glycogenesis and glycogenolysis were both strongly active, energy would be wasted. Reciprocal control ensures storage in the fed state and mobilization in fasting/exercise.

questions.questionText: Which statement best summarizes liver versus muscle glycogen fate?
options: Liver glycogen can become blood glucose; muscle glycogen becomes local glycolytic fuel
Muscle glycogen becomes blood glucose; liver glycogen becomes lactate only
Both are used only for local muscle ATP
questions.explanation: Liver has glucose-6-phosphatase and releases glucose into blood. Muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase and uses G6P for its own energy production.

questions.questionText: Which integrated statement best summarizes glycogen metabolism?
options: Glycogen stores glucose as a branched polymer; glycogenesis uses UDP-glucose and synthase, while glycogenolysis uses phosphorylase and debranching enzyme under reciprocal regulation
Glycogen is a linear fructose polymer degraded only by hydrolysis
Muscle glycogen mainly maintains blood glucose through glucose-6-phosphatase
Glycogenesis and glycogenolysis are exact reverse pathways using identical enzymes
questions.explanation: This combines the main points of the topic: glycogen structure, synthesis, degradation, and regulation. Glycogen metabolism is designed for rapid storage and rapid mobilization depending on fed, fasting, or exercise states.
